frames.caption: Cast n = 11 to uint so the right-fill / sign bit is plain data, then count = 0. x = 00001011 has set bits to clear.
frames.note: Casting to uint matters for the shift-and-mask variant (a signed >> would sign-extend forever on a negative n), and it makes the high bit ordinary data here too. For 11 the sign bit is 0, so the value is just the familiar 0000 1011. We will now peel its 1-bits off one at a time.

frames.caption: x = 00001011 != 0: enter the loop. The lowest set bit is bit 0 (array slot 7) — it is about to be cleared by x &= x - 1.
frames.note: The whole trick is x & (x - 1). Subtracting 1 flips the lowest set bit to 0 and turns every 0 below it into 1; AND-ing that against x therefore wipes out the lowest 1 AND the run of zeros beneath it, while leaving every higher bit untouched. So each pass removes EXACTLY one set bit — never more, never fewer.

frames.caption: Lowest set bit cleared -> x = 00001010; count = 1.
frames.note: Because every pass clears precisely one 1-bit, the loop body runs once per set bit, i.e. O(popcount) — for a sparse value like 11 (three 1s) that is 3 iterations, not the 32 a bit-by-bit scan would cost. count just tallies the passes.

frames.caption: x = 00001010 != 0: enter the loop. The lowest set bit is bit 1 (array slot 6) — it is about to be cleared by x &= x - 1.
frames.note: The whole trick is x & (x - 1). Subtracting 1 flips the lowest set bit to 0 and turns every 0 below it into 1; AND-ing that against x therefore wipes out the lowest 1 AND the run of zeros beneath it, while leaving every higher bit untouched. So each pass removes EXACTLY one set bit — never more, never fewer.

frames.caption: Lowest set bit cleared -> x = 00001000; count = 2.
frames.note: Because every pass clears precisely one 1-bit, the loop body runs once per set bit, i.e. O(popcount) — for a sparse value like 11 (three 1s) that is 3 iterations, not the 32 a bit-by-bit scan would cost. count just tallies the passes.

frames.caption: x = 00001000 != 0: enter the loop. The lowest set bit is bit 3 (array slot 4) — it is about to be cleared by x &= x - 1.
frames.note: The whole trick is x & (x - 1). Subtracting 1 flips the lowest set bit to 0 and turns every 0 below it into 1; AND-ing that against x therefore wipes out the lowest 1 AND the run of zeros beneath it, while leaving every higher bit untouched. So each pass removes EXACTLY one set bit — never more, never fewer.

frames.caption: Lowest set bit cleared -> x = 00000000; count = 3.
frames.note: Because every pass clears precisely one 1-bit, the loop body runs once per set bit, i.e. O(popcount) — for a sparse value like 11 (three 1s) that is 3 iterations, not the 32 a bit-by-bit scan would cost. count just tallies the passes.

frames.caption: x == 0, so the loop exits. Every original 1-bit was cleared in its own pass — return count = 3.
frames.note: x reaching 0 means there are no set bits left to remove, so the loop terminates after exactly as many passes as there were 1-bits. The popcount of 11 is 3. Both this Brian Kernighan loop and the 32-step shift-and-mask are O(1) per call (bounded by the word width), but this one only pays for the bits that are actually set.
